model_narrative: We are choosing which cells of a three by three by three cube to mark as selected, and we assign a numeric line value to each admissible line through the cube. Each cell is either selected or not. Each line value is a nonnegative quantity attached to one line description. We also track a single overall total that aggregates all of the line values. The goal is to make that overall total as small as possible.

sets.name: I
sets.doc: the three coordinate positions along one axis of a three by three by three cube; a cell of the cube is identified by a triple of these positions, one per axis

sets.name: S
sets.doc: the labels available for a line description along one axis; a, b, and c are the three fixed positions, while incr and decr are the two ordering directions

sets.name: B
sets.doc: the two bound sides used to sandwich each line value from below and from above

sets.name: X
sets.doc: the subset of labels that name a single fixed position rather than a direction

sets.name: D
sets.doc: the subset of labels that name an ordering direction rather than a fixed position

sets.name: LD
sets.members: 49 admissible label triples (s, sp, spp) over S, each describing one line of the cube; only triples that contain at least one incr direction are admissible
sets.doc: the set of valid line descriptions, each a triple of labels for the three axes; membership is restricted so that every admissible description involves at least one increasing direction, which keeps the line descriptions oriented consistently

params.name: ls
params.doc: the sign multiplier applied to a count of selected cells on each bound side; low uses one and high uses minus one, so the two sides bracket the count from below and above

params.name: lr
params.doc: the constant slack added on the right side for each bound side, in the same units as a line value; low uses two and high uses minus one

params.name: df
params.doc: the position offset that a label contributes at a given coordinate position; for the fixed labels a, b, c it is the signed distance from that label's position to the current position, for incr it is zero, and for decr it is a downward stepping offset; offsets are used to walk from a coordinate position to the cube cell that lies on the described line

vars.name: core
vars.doc: one if the cube cell at the given triple of coordinate positions is selected, zero otherwise

vars.name: line
vars.doc: the line value assigned to each admissible line description, large enough to dominate the selected cells lying on that line

vars.name: num
vars.doc: the total of all line values across every admissible line description

constraints.name: core[i,
constraints.intent: Exactly thirteen of the cube cells must be selected. Add up the selection indicators over every cell of the cube and require that total to equal thirteen.

constraints.name: I:
constraints.intent: For each admissible line description and for each of the two bound sides, the line value must be large enough to bracket the cells that the description picks out along that line. Walk each coordinate position along the line by applying the offset that each of the three labels contributes at that position, keep only the cube cells whose stepped coordinates stay inside the cube, and add up the selection indicators of those cells. On a given bound side, multiply that count by the side's sign multiplier and require the result to be at most the line value for the description plus the side's constant slack. If no cell on the line stays inside the cube, the requirement does not apply for that description.

constraints.name: num
constraints.intent: The overall total must equal the sum of the line values. Add up the line value across every admissible line description and set the overall total equal to that sum.

query: There's a proposal to keep the base of the structure light — no more than two cells in the bottom layer (i=1). Layer that cap in and tell me what the new line-imbalance comes out to.
expected_pyomo: model.bottom_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core[1, j, k] for j in model.I for k in model.I) <= 2)

query: The eight corner positions are being reserved for mounting hardware, so they have to stay clear. With all eight corners of the cube banned from holding a cell, what line-imbalance does the optimizer land on?
expected_pyomo: model.no_corners = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core[i, j, k] for i in [1, 3] for j in [1, 3] for k in [1, 3]) <= 0)

query: We're evaluating a top-light variant where the top layer barely participates — at most one cell up in layer i=3. Apply that and let me see how the total imbalance shifts.
expected_pyomo: model.top_light = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core[3, j, k] for j in model.I for k in model.I) <= 1)

query: There's a proposal to thin out the central vertical slice — the plane where the second coordinate is 2 — to no more than two cells. What does the line-imbalance come out to with that slice limited?
expected_pyomo: model.slice_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core[i, 2, k] for i in model.I for k in model.I) <= 2)

query: The middle layer of the optimal cube looks crowded. Shouldn't the central layer (i=2) hold no more than two cells so the structure stays balanced top-to-bottom? What's the model seeing that makes it lean on the middle so much?
expected_pyomo: model.mid_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core[2, j, k] for j in model.I for k in model.I) <= 2)

query: The plan packs several cells into the bottom layer. Couldn't we keep that base almost empty — at most one cell down there — and push the structure upward instead? What's the tradeoff that's pulling cells into the bottom?
expected_pyomo: model.bottom_sparse = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core[1, j, k] for j in model.I for k in model.I) <= 1)

query: The optimal layout leans heavily on the cube's eight outer corners, and that feels like it's gaming the geometry. Shouldn't we let at most one of those corner positions be used at all, so the placement has to stand on its interior structure? What's the model seeing in those corners that makes it want to load nearly all of them?
expected_pyomo: model.corner_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.core[i, j, k] for i in [1, 3] for j in [1, 3] for k in [1, 3]) <= 1)
